g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore what an Operating System does - the most fundamental software on any computer.
g.text: Understanding the Heart of Computer Systems

g.data-narration: The OS acts as a bridge between you and the computer hardware, making complex hardware easy to use.
An OS has two key perspectives: as an extended virtual machine and as a resource manager allocating hardware.
g.text: Programmers use simple commands, not hardware details

g.data-narration: The OS performs four main functions: Process, Memory, File System, and Device Management.
g.text: The Four Core Functions of an Operating System

g.data-narration: Process Management handles CPU scheduling - deciding which program runs when, creating the illusion of multitasking.
The OS uses algorithms like Round Robin and First-Come-First-Served to fairly allocate CPU time to programs.
g.text: - Round Robin - Time slices for each process

g.data-narration: Memory Management allocates RAM to programs, protects each process's memory space, and uses virtual memory for larger programs.
Virtual Memory extends RAM using disk space, letting programs use more memory than physically available through swapping.
g.text: OS transparently moves data between RAM and disk

g.data-narration: File System Management organizes data into files and folders, providing logical storage abstraction over physical disks.
Device Management handles input/output devices through drivers, buffering data flow between fast CPU and slow peripherals.
The OS uses buffering to smooth speed differences, caching to store frequently accessed data, and spooling to queue print jobs.
OS provides essential services: User Interface, Program Execution, I/O Operations, and File System manipulation.
The Kernel is the core of the OS, always in memory. Applications request OS services through system calls - the fundamental interface.
In summary: OS is both an extended machine hiding hardware complexity and a resource manager ensuring fair, efficient system operation.
g.text: Summary: OS - Extended Machine and Resource Manager
Four Functions: Process, Memory, File System, Device Management | Key Concept: Abstraction
